blocks:
Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (١)
Say, ‘O faithless ones! (1)
لَآ أَعْبُدُ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٢)
I do not worship what you worship, (2)
وَلَآ أَنتُمْ عَـٰبِدُونَ مَآ أَعْبُدُ (٣)
nor do you worship what I worship; (3)
وَلَآ أَنَا۠ عَابِدٌ مَّا عَبَدتُّمْ (٤)
nor will I worship what you have worshipped, (4)
وَلَآ أَنتُمْ عَـٰبِدُونَ مَآ أَعْبُدُ (٥)
nor will you worship what I worship. (5)
لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِىَ دِينِ (٦)
To you your religion, and to me my religion.’ (6)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Kafirun"

This Surah was revealed in "Mecca," and its tone and content are clear witnesses to this fact. Furthermore, the circumstances of its revelation—which, God willing, will be mentioned later—serve as another proof for this claim. The possibility suggested by some that it might be Medinan seems very remote.

The tone of the Surah indicates it was revealed at a time when Muslims were in the minority and the disbelievers were in the majority, and the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was under severe pressure from them. They were insistent on drawing him toward a compromise with polytheism (shirk). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله rejected all of them and made them completely despair, without seeking to engage in a physical conflict with them.

This is a model for all Muslims: under no circumstances should they compromise with enemies regarding the foundations of religion and Islam. Whenever such a desire is expressed by the enemies, they should be made completely hopeless. Specifically, in this Surah, it is emphasized twice that "I do not worship your deities," and this emphasis is to make them despair. Likewise, it is emphasized again that "You will never worship my deity, the One God," which is a proof of their stubbornness, and its conclusion is: "Me and my monotheistic faith, and you and your decayed, polytheistic faith!" Regarding the virtue of this Surah, numerous narrations have been cited that indicate the extraordinary importance of its content, including:

In a hadith (narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله), he said:

من قرأ قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ‌ فكانما قرأ ربع القرآن و تباعدت عنه مردة الشياطين، و برأ من الشرك، و يعافى من الفزع الاكبر:

"Whoever recites 'Say: O disbelievers,' it is as if he has recited a quarter of the Quran; the rebellious satans will distance themselves from him, he will be purified from polytheism, and he will be safe from the Great Terror (of the Day of Resurrection)." [^1] The expression "a quarter of the Quran" is perhaps because about one-fourth of the Quran deals with the struggle against polytheism and idol-worship, and its essence is contained in this Surah. The distancing of rebellious satans is because in this Surah, the polytheists are firmly rejected, and we know that polytheism is the most important tool of Satan.

Salvation on the Day of Resurrection is also primarily dependent on monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of polytheism—the very subject around which this Surah revolves.

In another hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "A man came to him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! I have come so that you may teach me something to recite when I go to sleep.' He said:

اذا اخذت مضجعك فاقرأ قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ‌ ثم نم على خاتمتها فانها براءة من الشرك:

'When you go to your bed, recite the Surah "O disbelievers," then sleep at its conclusion, for it is a disavowal of polytheism.'" ^2 It is also narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله that he said to "Jubayr ibn Mut'im": "Would you like to be among the best of your companions in terms of provisions and supplies when you go on a journey? He said: 'Yes, may my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah!' He said: 'Recite these five Surahs: "قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ‌", "إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ وَ الْفَتْحُ‌", "قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ", "قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ الْفَلَقِ‌", and "قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ النَّاسِ‌", and begin your recitation with "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌".'" [^1] And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he used to say: "My father would say that 'Say: O disbelievers' is a quarter of the Quran, and when he finished it, he would say: 'I worship Allah alone, I worship Allah alone' (اعبد اللَّه وحده، اعبد اللَّه وحده)." ^2

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 551.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ (1) لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ (2) وَ لا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ (3) وَ لا أَنا عابِدٌ ما عَبَدْتُّمْ (4)

وَ لا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ (5) لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَ لِيَ دِينِ (6)

Circumstances of Revelation

It is mentioned in narrations that "this Surah was revealed concerning a group of the leaders of the polytheists of Quraysh, such as 'Walid ibn Mughirah,' 'As ibn Wa'il,' 'Harith ibn Qays,' 'Umayyah ibn Khalaf,' and others. They said: 'O Muhammad! Come and follow our religion, and we will follow your religion, and we will make you a partner in all our privileges. For one year, you worship our gods, and the next year, we will worship your God. If your religion is better, we have shared in it with you and taken our portion, and if our religion is better, you have shared in our religion and taken your portion!' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'I seek refuge in Allah from associating anything as a partner with Him!' They said: 'At least touch some of our gods and seek blessings from them, and we will believe you and worship your God!' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'I am waiting for the command of my Lord.'

At this moment, the Surah 'Say: O disbelievers' was revealed. The Messenger of Allah came to the Masjid al-Haram while a group of the leaders of Quraysh were gathered there. He stood over them and recited this Surah to the end. When they heard the message of this Surah, they became completely hopeless and began to harass the Prophet and his companions." [^1]

[^1]: This circumstance of revelation has been cited by many commentators with slight variations in wording, including "Tabarsi" in "Majma' al-Bayan," "Qurtubi" in his Tafsir, "Abu al-Futuh Razi" in his Tafsir, and "Suyuti" in "al-Durr al-Manthur."

Commentary

I Shall Never Compromise with Idol-Worshippers

The verses of this Surah address the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying:

"Say: O disbelievers" (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الْكافِرُونَ‌).

"I do not worship what you worship" (لا أَعْبُدُ ما تَعْبُدُونَ‌).

"Nor are you worshippers of what I worship" (وَ لا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ). In this way, He distinguishes his path completely from theirs and explicitly says: I will never practice idol-worship, and you too—with this stubbornness you possess, your blind imitation of your ancestors which you insist upon, and the abundant illegitimate interests you gain from idol-worshippers—are never ready for pure monotheism free from polytheism.

Once again, to completely discourage the idol-worshippers from any kind of compromise between monotheism and idol-worship, He adds: "Nor will I ever be a worshipper of what you have worshipped" (وَ لا أَنا عابِدٌ ما عَبَدْتُّمْ‌).

"Nor will you be worshippers of what I worship" (وَ لا أَنْتُمْ عابِدُونَ ما أَعْبُدُ). Therefore, do not make futile insistences on a compromise regarding the issue of idol-worship, for this matter is impossible.

"Since it is so, your religion is for you, and my religion is for me" (لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَ لِيَ دِينِ‌). Many commentators have stated that "the disbelievers" (al-Kafirun) here refers to a specific group of the leaders of the Meccan idol-worshippers. Therefore, the "Alif and Lam" in "al-Kafirun" is for "specification" (ahd), not for "generality" (jam').

Their reason for this, in addition to what was mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, might be that many of the Meccan idol-worshippers eventually embraced faith. Therefore, if He says "neither do you worship my deity nor do I worship yours," it must refer to that group of the leaders of polytheism and disbelief who never believed until the end of their lives; otherwise, many polytheists entered Islam in crowds during the Conquest of Mecca.

Here, several questions arise that must be answered

1- Why does the Surah begin with the command "Qul" (Say)?

Would it not have been better to say "O disbelievers" without "Say" at the beginning? In other words, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must carry out God's command and say the sentence "O disbelievers" to them, not repeat the word "Say" as well.

The answer to this question is clear considering the content of the Surah, because the Arab polytheists had invited the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to compromise over the idols. He had to negate this from himself and say: I will never submit to you, and I will not pollute my worship with polytheism. If the word "Say" were not at the beginning of this Surah, the speech would be the speech of God, and in that case, the sentence "I do not worship what you worship" and its like would not make sense.

Furthermore, since the word "Say" was in Gabriel's message from God, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is obliged to repeat it exactly to preserve the authenticity of the Quran. This itself shows that Gabriel and the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not make the slightest change in conveying the divine revelation, and they practically proved they are servants obedient to the divine command, just as we read in Surah Yunus, verse 15: قُلْ ما يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُ مِنْ تِلْقاءِ نَفْسِي إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيَ‌: "Say: It is not for me to change it of my own accord. I only follow what is revealed to me."

2- Did the idol-worshippers deny God?

We know that the idol-worshippers never denied God, and according to the explicit verses of the Quran, if they were asked about the Creator of the heavens and the earth, they would say: It is Allah:

وَ لَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ‌ (Luqman: 25).

So how does it say in this Surah: "Neither do I worship your deity nor do you worship mine"?

The answer to this question also becomes clear considering that the discussion is not about the issue of creation, but rather about the issue of worship. The idol-worshippers considered the Creator of the world to be "Allah," but they believed that idols must be "worshipped" so that they might become intermediaries before God, or that we are not worthy at all to worship God, but rather we must worship physical idols. It is here that the Quran draws a red line through their delusions and fantasies, saying that worship must be for Allah alone—not idols, and not both!

3- What is the purpose of this repetition?

There is much discussion regarding why the negation of worshipping idols by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the negation of worshipping God by the polytheists is repeated.

A group believes that this repetition is for emphasis and to completely discourage the polytheists, to separate their path from the path of Islam, and to prove the impossibility of compromise between monotheism and polytheism. In other words, because they were insistent and repetitive in inviting the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله toward polytheism, the Quran also repeats its rejection of them.

In a hadith, it is mentioned that "Abu Shakir al-Daysani" (one of the atheists of Imam al-Sadiq's era) asked one of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq named "Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal" (Muhammad ibn Ali Nu'mani Kufi, known as Mu'min al-Taq) about the reason for the repetition of these verses, saying: "Is it possible for a wise person to have such repetition in his speech?"

Abu Ja'far al-Ahwal, having no answer at that moment, entered Medina and came to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام to ask about this. The Imam عليه السلام said: "The reason for the revelation of these verses and their repetition was that the Quraysh proposed to the Messenger of Allah: 'For one year you worship our gods, and the next year we will worship your God, and likewise the following year you worship our gods and the next year (the fourth year) we will worship your God.' The above verses were revealed and negated all these proposals."

When Abu Ja'far "al-Ahwal" explained this answer to "Abu Shakir," he said: "هذا ما حمله الإبل من الحجاز!": "This is a load that the camels have brought from Hijaz!" [^1] (implying that these are not your words but the words of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام).

Others have said: This repetition is because one refers to the present state and the other refers to the future; meaning, neither now nor in the future will I ever worship your deity! But apparently, there is no evidence for this interpretation.

A third interpretation has also been given for this repetition: that the "first" expresses the difference in deities, and the "second" expresses the difference in worship. That is, neither do I ever worship your deities, nor is the manner of my worship like yours, because my worship is sincere and free from any polytheism.

Furthermore, your worship of idols is out of blind imitation of ancestors, while my worship of God is out of research and gratitude. ^2 But the apparent meaning is that this repetition is for emphasis, as we explained above and as was indicated in the hadith of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام.

There is also a fourth interpretation here, which is that in the second verse He says: "I do not worship what you are worshipping now," and in the fourth verse He says: "I did not worship your deities in the past either, let alone today."

This difference, considering that in the second verse "ta'budun" is in the present tense and "abadtum" in the fourth verse is in the past tense, does not seem remote [^1], although this interpretation only solves the repetition of the second and fourth verses, while the repetition of the third and fifth verses remains as it was. ^2

4- Does the concept of the verse "Your religion is for you..." permit idol-worship?!

Sometimes it has been imagined that the last verse of this Surah, which says: "Your religion is for you, and my religion is for me," has the same concept as "universal peace" and allows them to remain on their religion, because it does not insist on the acceptance of the religion of Islam! But this notion is very weak and baseless, because the tone of the verses clearly shows that this expression is a type of humiliation and threat; meaning, may your religion be yours! And soon you will see its disastrous consequences. This is similar to what appears in Surah al-Qasas, verse 55:

وَ إِذا سَمِعُوا اللَّغْوَ أَعْرَضُوا عَنْهُ وَ قالُوا لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَ لَكُمْ أَعْمالُكُمْ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ لا نَبْتَغِي الْجاهِلِينَ‌: "And when they hear vain talk, they turn away from it and say: 'Our deeds are for us and your deeds are for you. Peace be upon you (a peace of farewell and separation); we do not seek the ignorant!'" A clear witness to this fact is the hundreds of verses of the Holy Quran that strike down polytheism in all its forms, consider it the most hated of all acts, and deem it an unforgivable sin.

[^1]: "Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim," Vol. 2, p. 445. ^2: According to this interpretation, "ma" in the second and third verses is "relative" (mawsulah), and in the fourth and fifth verses it is "infinitive" (masdariyah). (Abu al-Futuh Razi mentioned this as one of the interpretations in Vol. 12, p. 192, and the late Tabarsi also referred to it under the verses in question). [^1]: Therefore, "abid," which is a subject noun (ism fa'il), must also give a past meaning in this verse. ^2: Additionally, it should be noted that although "ma mawsulah" is mostly used for non-rational beings, it is frequently seen to be used for rational beings as well; the verses of the Quran are witnesses to this claim.

Other answers have also been given to this question, such as that the verse has an omitted part and is estimated as follows: "For you is the reward of your religion and for me is the reward of my religion."

Another is that "din" here means "recompense," and the verse has nothing omitted, and its meaning is: you will receive your recompense and I will receive mine. [^1] But the first interpretation and answer seem more appropriate.

5- He never compromised with polytheism for a single moment

What is contained in this Surah is actually an expression of the reality that monotheism and polytheism are two contradictory programs and two completely separate paths, and they have no similarity to one another. Monotheism connects man to God, while polytheism makes him a stranger to God.

Monotheism is the secret of unity and oneness in all fields, while polytheism is the cause of division and scattering in all affairs.

Monotheism elevates man from the world of matter and the realm of nature and links him to the infinite Divine existence beyond nature, while polytheism plunges man into the pit of nature and links him to limited, weak, and mortal beings.

For this reason, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and all the great prophets not only did not compromise with polytheism for a single moment, but their first and most important program was to fight against it.

[^1]: Additionally, it should be noted that "din" in the phrase "wa liya din" is marked with a kasra, and its kasra indicates an omitted "ya," and it is actually "wa liya dini."

Today, all seekers of the path of truth, scholars, and preachers of this faith must continue this same line, and everywhere declare their disavowal and disgust for any kind of polytheism and compromise with polytheists.

This is the authentic path of Islam.

O Allah! Keep us away from all kinds of polytheism and polytheistic thoughts and actions. O Lord! The whispers of the polytheists of our era are also dangerous; protect us from falling into their trap. O God! Grant us such courage, clarity, and decisiveness that, like the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we may reject any proposal of compromise with disbelief and polytheism.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Kafirun.
